school: Abu Musa Jabir ibn Hayyan (c. 721–815), known in Europe as Geber, is considered the father of Islamic alchemy and early chemistry. A student of Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq, he introduced systematic experimental methods.
quotes: The first essential in chemistry is that you should perform practical work and conduct experiments — for he who performs not practical work nor makes experiments will never attain the least degree of mastery.
Work, work, and yet more work is the only key to the secrets of nature.

school: Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi (c. 780–850), who founded algebra as a discipline. His Kitab al-Jabr gave algebra its name, and his work on Indian numerals introduced the decimal system to Europe. The word 'algorithm' derives from his name.
quotes: I have composed this work on calculation by completion and reduction, confining it to what is easiest and most useful — such as men constantly require in cases of inheritance, legacies, and trade.
The numbers required in calculation are of three kinds: roots, squares, and simple numbers.

school: Abbasid Scientific Court — most influential Islamic astrologer; his Latin translations shaped European medieval astronomy
bio: Persian Islamic astronomer, astrologer, and mathematician whose works significantly influenced European medieval science through astronomical and astrological treatises.

school: Abbasid astronomer (c. 805–870) who worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad. His Elements of Astronomy became the most widely read astronomical textbook in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe, where it was translated into Latin by John of Seville. Dante’s cosmology in the Divine Comedy draws directly from Al-Farghani’s work.
quotes: The science of the stars is among the most noble — for it concerns itself with the greatest of God's creations.
The circumference of the earth, when measured, reveals the precision of its Creator.
bio: Persian astronomer of the 9th century known for his comprehensive astronomical treatise that became influential in both Islamic and European science. His work on celestial spheres was widely studied and translated.

school: Three brothers — Muhammad, Ahmad, and al-Hasan ibn Musa (9th century) — who worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad. They patronised translators, funded scientific expeditions, and wrote the Book of Ingenious Devices describing over 100 mechanical inventions including automatic fountains, self-trimming lamps, and programmable musical machines.
bio: Three Persian brothers of the 9th century who were mathematicians, astronomers, and patrons of science. They made significant contributions to geometry, astronomy, and mechanics, and played a key role in the House of Wisdom.

school: Nestorian Christian physician and translator (809–873) who led the translation movement at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad. He and his school translated virtually the entire Greek medical and scientific corpus into Arabic, including Galen, Hippocrates, and Plato. Without his work, much of ancient Greek knowledge would have been lost to history.
bio: A renowned Arab physician, translator, and ophthalmologist of the 9th century who made significant contributions to medicine and translated Greek scientific texts into Arabic and Syriac. He is considered one of the most important medical figures of the Islamic Golden Age.

school: Melkite Christian physician, translator, and polymath (820–912) who worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad. He translated Greek scientific and philosophical texts into Arabic and wrote original works on optics, astronomy, and medicine. His treatise on the distinction between spirit and soul influenced both Islamic and Latin scholastic philosophy.
bio: A 9th-century Arab physician, mathematician, and astronomer from Baalbek known for his translations of Greek works and original contributions to medicine and mathematics. He was a prominent figure in the transmission of ancient knowledge to the Islamic world.

books.title: On the Difference Between Spirit and Soul

school: Sabian mathematician, astronomer, and physician (826–901) who worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad. He translated and improved upon Euclid, Archimedes, and Apollonius, and made original contributions to number theory and the calculation of the volume of paraboloids. He founded a dynasty of scholars that served the Abbasid caliphs for generations.
bio: A 9th-century Arab mathematician, astronomer, physician, and philosopher from Harran who made fundamental contributions to geometry, astronomy, and medicine. He was a prolific translator and original thinker who significantly advanced Islamic scientific knowledge.

school: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi (854–925), known as Rhazes, the greatest clinician of the medieval Islamic world. His al-Hawi compiled the entire medical knowledge of Greek, Indian, and Islamic traditions. He first distinguished between smallpox and measles.
quotes: The most excellent doctor is he who can bring hope to the patient’s heart.
Truth in medicine is a goal that cannot be fully attained — the art is long and life is short.
I prefer to verify by experiment rather than accept authority alone.
The most excellent doctor is he who can bring hope to the patient’s heart.
Truth in medicine is a goal that cannot be fully attained — the art is long and life is short.
I prefer to verify by experiment rather than accept authority alone.

school: A ninth- and tenth-century astronomer and mathematician from Harran, he refined Ptolemy's astronomical observations with unprecedented accuracy, determining the length of the solar year to within two minutes of the modern value. His Kitab al-Zij (Astronomical Tables) corrected several of Ptolemy's calculations and introduced the use of trigonometric functions into astronomy. His work was translated into Latin and profoundly influenced medieval European astronomers including Copernicus.
quotes: The motions of the heavens are governed by laws as fixed as the laws of arithmetic.
Observation corrects theory — and theory without observation is merely poetry.

school: Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi (903–986), known in the Latin West as Azophi, was one of the most important astronomers in Islamic history. Based in Isfahan and later at the Buyid court in Shiraz, his masterwork Kitab Suwar al-Kawakib al-Thabita (Book of Fixed Stars) revised and updated Ptolemy's star catalogue with original observations, correcting star positions and magnitudes and adding Arabic star names that are still used today (such as Aldebaran, Altair, and Fomalhaut). He was the first astronomer in recorded history to describe the Andromeda Galaxy, which he called 'a small cloud,' and the first to observe the Large Magellanic Cloud. His star charts combined scientific precision with artistic beauty and were copied for centuries across the Islamic world and medieval Europe.
bio: Persian astronomer of the 10th century known for his detailed star catalog and observations of celestial phenomena. His work remained the most comprehensive star catalog in the Islamic world for centuries.

school: A tenth-century Andalusian physician and surgeon from Cordoba, he authored Kitab al-Tasrif (The Method of Medicine), a thirty-volume medical encyclopaedia that became the standard surgical reference in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe. The surgical volume described over two hundred instruments, many of which he designed himself, and covered procedures from cauterisation to cataract surgery. He is widely regarded as the father of modern surgery.
quotes: The root and foundation of medicine is compassion for the patient — the physician who lacks it should not be trusted.
I have explained the art of surgery in detail never before attempted by any physician before me.

school: Cairo — Founded empirical optics and the experimental method; influenced Roger Bacon, Kepler and Newton
quotes: The duty of the man who investigates the writings of scientists is to make himself an enemy of all that he reads.
The duty of the man who investigates the writings of scientists, if learning the truth is his goal, is to make himself an enemy of all that he reads.
Truth is sought for its own sake. Those engaged in the quest for truth are not interested in what they already presume to know.
The seeker after truth is not one who studies the writings of the ancients and follows his natural inclination — but one who suspects his own faith in them.
If learning the truth is the scientist's goal, then he must make himself the enemy of all that he reads.

school: Abu Rayhan al-Biruni (973–1048), a polymath who wrote 146 works. His Kitab al-Hind is the first systematic study of Indian civilisation by an outsider. He calculated the earth's radius to within 16 km of the modern value.

books.title: Alberuni's India (Kitab al-Hind)
books.note: Classic study of Indian civilization — English

quotes: I shall take refuge in the character of a chronicler, and describe what I found, without adding any judgment of my own.
Facts do not perish, even when those who carry them pass away.
We must not allow the love of what is familiar to blind us to what is true.

school: An eleventh-century Andalusian astronomer and instrument maker from Toledo, he developed the universal astrolabe (the Saphaea), which could be used at any latitude, a significant advance over earlier designs. He compiled the Toledan Tables, the most accurate astronomical tables of his era, which were used across Europe for over two centuries. His observations of the motion of the solar apogee were the first to demonstrate that it moves slowly relative to the fixed stars.
quotes: I corrected Ptolemy not out of disrespect — but because the observations demanded it.
The Toledo Tables are my gift to every navigator who will come after me.
Precision in astronomy is precision in worship — for we are describing God’s creation.

school: A twelfth-century Andalusian physician from Seville, he came from a distinguished family of physicians and is known for his clinical text al-Taysir fi al-Mudawat wa-l-Tadbir (The Facilitation of Treatment). He was among the first physicians to describe conditions such as pericarditis and mediastinal tumours, and he advocated for experimental methods in medicine. His close collaboration with the philosopher Ibn Rushd (Averroes) produced complementary works on medical theory and practice.
quotes: The physician who treats himself has a fool for a patient.
Clinical observation is the only trustworthy foundation for the art of medicine.
The physician who treats himself has a fool for a patient.
Clinical observation is the only trustworthy foundation for the art of medicine.

school: Badi' al-Zaman al-Jazari (1136–1206), chief engineer at the Artuqid court who wrote the Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices, describing 50 mechanisms including programmable automata and the earliest known crankshaft.
quotes: The craftsman who understands the principles behind his craft will surpass the one who merely follows instructions.
Every machine is a kind of philosophy — it embodies a theory of how things work.
The greatest invention is the one that reduces human burden without reducing human dignity.

school: A thirteenth-century Andalusian botanist and pharmacologist from Malaga, he authored Kitab al-Jami (The Comprehensive Book on Simple Drugs), the largest and most comprehensive botanical and pharmaceutical encyclopaedia of the medieval period. The work catalogued over 1,400 plants, drugs, and foods, drawing on personal fieldwork conducted across North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean. His systematic approach to botanical classification and drug testing represented a major advance in pharmacological science.
quotes: God has placed in every plant a remedy — the physician’s task is to find it.
I have travelled every land not for adventure but for knowledge of what grows in it.
The herbalist who does not travel is like a scholar who does not read beyond one book.

school: Islamic Science and Philosophy — survived the Mongol invasions by serving Hulagu Khan, then established the Maragha Observatory (1259), the world's greatest scientific institution of its era
quotes: Knowledge is the greatest of all fortresses — it neither crumbles nor is captured.
The universe obeys its own laws, which we must struggle to understand.

school: A thirteenth-century Syrian physician and polymath based in Cairo, he is best known for his discovery of pulmonary circulation, describing how blood passes from the right to the left ventricle of the heart via the lungs, three centuries before William Harvey. He detailed this finding in his Sharh Tashrih al-Qanun (Commentary on the Anatomy of the Canon), challenging Galen's long-accepted model of the cardiovascular system. He was also a prolific author on jurisprudence, theology, and philosophy.
quotes: The blood must have a means of passage to the left cavity — and that passage is through the lung.
I have not omitted anything that might benefit the student of medicine, nor added anything that could mislead him.

school: Maragha school — student of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi; explained the rainbow; major work on optics, astronomy and medicine
bio: A Persian polymath of the 13th-14th centuries who made significant contributions to astronomy, mathematics, medicine, and philosophy. He wrote commentaries on major scientific works and developed astronomical models.

school: A thirteenth- and fourteenth-century Moroccan mathematician and astronomer from Marrakesh, he authored numerous works on arithmetic, algebra, and astronomy. His Talkhis A'mal al-Hisab (Summary of Arithmetic Operations) introduced innovative methods for calculation and became a standard mathematical textbook across North Africa. He also contributed to the development of algebraic notation and compiled astronomical tables used in the western Islamic world.
bio: A Moroccan mathematician and astronomer of the 13th-14th centuries known for his works on algebra, arithmetic, and astronomy. He made contributions to mathematical notation and astronomical calculations.

school: Kamal al-Din Hasan ibn Ali al-Farisi (1267–1318) was a Persian mathematician and physicist who produced the first correct scientific explanation of the rainbow. Working in Tabriz under the patronage of the Ilkhanid court, he was a student of the great astronomer Qutb al-Din al-Shirazi. His masterwork Tanqih al-Manazir (Revision of Optics) was a commentary on Ibn al-Haytham's Kitab al-Manazir that went far beyond the original. Using a glass sphere filled with water to model a raindrop, al-Farisi demonstrated that a rainbow is produced by two refractions and one internal reflection of sunlight inside raindrops — the same explanation accepted by modern physics. He also contributed to number theory, producing an original proof related to amicable numbers.
quotes: Light bends when it enters water — and in that bending is the secret of the rainbow.
I confirmed Ibn al-Haytham’s optics by experiment — confirmation is the honour due to good science.
The colours of the rainbow are not painted by chance — they are a proof of mathematical order.

school: Persian mathematician and astronomer (c. 1380–1429) who worked at Ulugh Beg’s observatory in Samarkand. He calculated pi to sixteen decimal places — a record that stood for over 150 years — and developed methods for extracting roots of arbitrary degree. His Key of Arithmetic became a standard reference across the Islamic world.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 15th century who made groundbreaking contributions to decimal fractions, trigonometry, and computational mathematics. He worked at the Samarkand observatory and advanced mathematical precision in calculations.

school: Ottoman-Timurid astronomer and mathematician (1403–1474) who worked at the Samarkand Observatory under Ulugh Beg before moving to Constantinople at the invitation of Mehmed the Conqueror. He challenged Ptolemaic astronomy and argued that the earth’s rotation could not be disproved by physics alone — a remarkable pre-Copernican insight.
quotes: The astronomer who does not question his own measurements has stopped being an astronomer.
Mathematics is the language in which God wrote the motion of the stars.

school: Ahmad ibn al-Hajj Muhiddin Piri, known as Piri Reis (1465–1553), was an Ottoman admiral and cartographer who created some of the most remarkable maps of the Age of Exploration. Born in Gallipoli, he sailed with his uncle Kemal Reis on naval campaigns across the Mediterranean before rising to command Ottoman fleets in the Red Sea and Indian Ocean. His surviving 1513 world map, drawn on gazelle skin, is one of the earliest maps to show the Americas and is notable for its accuracy in depicting the South American coastline — drawn partly from a lost map attributed to Columbus. His Kitab-i Bahriye (Book of Navigation) is a masterpiece of Ottoman maritime cartography containing detailed charts of the Mediterranean with sailing instructions and coastal descriptions. He was executed in 1553 after a failed naval campaign in the Persian Gulf.
quotes: The sea gives generously to those who respect her — and takes everything from those who do not.
Every map is a theory of the world — draw it well.
The navigator who does not know the stars has already lost his way before he sets sail.

school: Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ma'ruf al-Shami al-Asadi (1526–1585) was an Ottoman polymath — astronomer, engineer, clockmaker, and mathematician — who built the great Istanbul Observatory in 1577. Born in Damascus, he studied in Cairo before being appointed chief astronomer (müneccimbaşı) by Sultan Murad III. His observatory, equipped with instruments rivalling those of his contemporary Tycho Brahe, produced astronomical tables of remarkable accuracy. He also invented a steam turbine (described in his al-Turuq al-Saniyya) and designed mechanical clocks of unprecedented precision. His work on optics built on Ibn al-Haytham. The observatory was demolished in 1580 after a political backlash linked to superstitious fears — a catastrophic loss for Ottoman science. Despite this, Taqi al-Din's surviving manuscripts reveal a scientist of the first rank.
quotes: The observatory is not a place of arrogance — it is a place of submission to what the heavens reveal.
Every star that is measured glorifies God in the language of mathematics.
Precision is an act of piety — the scientist who is careless has not understood his task.

school: Baha al-Din al-Amili, known as Shaykh Bahai (1547–1621), the most versatile scholar of the Safavid era — simultaneously a leading jurist, mathematician, astronomer, architect, and poet. He designed Isfahan's Masjid-i Shah.
bio: A Persian polymath of the 16th-17th centuries known for his contributions to mathematics, astronomy, engineering, and Islamic sciences. He was a prominent theologian and scholar during the Safavid period.

school: Ottoman mathematics — the most accomplished Ottoman mathematician; introduced modern European mathematics to the Ottoman system
bio: Ottoman mathematician and astronomer of the 18th century known for his work in mathematics and reform of astronomical knowledge. He contributed to the advancement of mathematical sciences in the Ottoman Empire.

school: A Pakistani theoretical physicist who shared the 1979 Nobel Prize in Physics for his contribution to the electroweak unification theory, becoming the first Muslim Nobel laureate in science. He founded the International Centre for Theoretical Physics (ICTP) in Trieste, Italy, to provide scientists from developing countries access to advanced research facilities. His lifelong mission was to promote scientific excellence in the Muslim world, and he advocated tirelessly for investment in fundamental research.
bio: Pakistani theoretical physicist who won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1979 for his contributions to electroweak theory and was a pioneering Muslim scientist.
quotes: Science is the reflection of the Divine in the human mind

school: Abbas ibn Firnas was an Andalusian polymath, inventor, and poet at the court of the Umayyad emirate of Cordoba. He is best known for his attempt at heavier-than-air flight using a wing apparatus, predating modern aviation by a millennium. He also developed a process for manufacturing colourless glass, constructed a planetarium, and devised a water clock.
quotes: If the birds can master the air, then so can man — it requires only courage and ingenuity.
The laws of nature are the laws of God written in a language the patient observer can learn to read.

school: Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi was a Persian astronomer whose 'Kitab Suwar al-Kawakib' (Book of Fixed Stars) updated Ptolemy's star catalogue with original observations and Arabic star names. He made the first recorded observation of the Andromeda Galaxy, which he described as a 'small cloud.' Many star names in use today, such as Aldebaran and Betelgeuse, derive from his work.

Abu al-Wafa al-Buzjani was a Persian mathematician and astronomer who made foundational contributions to trigonometry. He introduced the tangent function, developed methods of calculating trigonometric tables, and established important trigonometric identities. His work on geometric constructions using a fixed compass opening was centuries ahead of its time.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 10th century who made major advances in trigonometry, geometry, and astronomy. He developed new trigonometric functions and improved astronomical observations.

school: Abu Zayd al-Balkhi was a Persian polymath who founded the 'Balkhi school' of geographic mapping and made remarkably early contributions to psychology. His 'Masalih al-Abdan wa al-Anfus' (Sustenance for Bodies and Souls) distinguished between neuroses and psychoses and proposed cognitive and behavioural treatments, anticipating modern clinical psychology by a millennium.
bio: Persian scholar of the 9th-10th centuries who made contributions to geography, mathematics, and medicine. He is known for his geographical works and treatises on various scientific subjects.

school: Abu Sahl al-Quhi was a Persian mathematician and astronomer who made important advances in the geometry of conic sections. He solved geometric problems that had been open since antiquity and developed new methods for determining centres of gravity. He directed the astronomical observatory in Baghdad under the Buyid emir Sharaf al-Dawla.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 10th century known for his contributions to geometry, algebra, and astronomical instruments. He worked on problems in solid geometry and the theory of proportions.

school: Abu Mahmud Khojandi was a Persian astronomer and mathematician from Khujand in Central Asia. He constructed an enormous mural sextant at Rayy to measure the obliquity of the ecliptic with unprecedented precision. He also contributed to number theory, working on problems related to the sum of two cubes.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 10th century known for his work on trigonometry and astronomy. He made contributions to spherical trigonometry and constructed astronomical instruments.

school: Abu Nasr Mansur ibn Ali ibn Iraq was a Persian mathematician and astronomer who was the teacher and later collaborator of al-Biruni. He made important contributions to spherical trigonometry, developing the law of sines for spherical triangles. His mathematical work provided essential tools for the astronomical calculations of the Islamic golden age.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer who made significant contributions to spherical trigonometry and astronomy during the 10th-11th centuries. He served under the Ghaznavid dynasty and is known for his work on trigonometric functions.

school: Abu Mansur al-Baghdadi was an Arab mathematician and Shafi'i-Ash'ari theologian. His mathematical works contributed to number theory, and he classified numbers in ways that anticipated later developments. He also authored important works on Islamic theology and the classification of religious sects, making him an unusual figure who excelled in both the rational and religious sciences.
bio: Medieval Islamic theologian and scholar known for his works on Islamic theology and refutations of theological positions he considered heretical.

school: Abd al-Latif al-Baghdadi was a physician, philosopher, traveller, and polymath. His 'Kitab al-Ifada' on Egypt contains precise anatomical observations that corrected Galen, including his discovery that the lower jaw is a single bone, not two. He studied under over 200 scholars and his systematic approach to empirical observation made him a remarkable figure in the history of science.
bio: Medieval Islamic physician and polymath from Baghdad known for his medical treatises, anatomical observations, and contributions to various sciences.

books.title: Book of the Examination of the Properties of Foodstuffs

school: Abu'l-Barakat al-Baghdadi was an Iraqi philosopher and physician whose 'Kitab al-Mu'tabar' challenged Aristotelian physics. He argued that a moving body has an inclination (mail) that decreases as it moves, anticipating aspects of the later impetus theory. His philosophical critiques of Avicenna's system were influential in the development of post-Avicennan Islamic philosophy.
bio: Persian philosopher and physician of the 11th-12th centuries who contributed to natural philosophy, medicine, and metaphysics. He challenged Aristotelian physics and proposed innovative theories about motion and causality.

school: Abu Kamil Shuja ibn Aslam was an Egyptian mathematician who built upon al-Khwarizmi's pioneering algebra. He introduced the systematic use of irrational numbers as solutions and coefficients, solved systems of equations, and applied algebra to geometry. His work directly influenced the European mathematician Fibonacci, who drew extensively on Abu Kamil's methods.
bio: Egyptian Islamic mathematician known for his advanced work in algebra and geometry during the 9th-10th centuries.

school: Abu Hanifa al-Dinawari was a Persian polymath whose 'Kitab al-Nabat' (Book of Plants) earned him recognition as the founder of Arabic botany. Describing over 637 plants with their growth stages and habitats, the work was the most comprehensive botanical treatise of the medieval Islamic world. He also authored important works on astronomy, mathematics, and history.
bio: Persian polymath of the 9th century known for his contributions to botany, mathematics, and history. He wrote treatises on plants and their properties.

school: Ahmad al-Qalqashandi was an Egyptian scholar who authored 'Subh al-A'sha,' a fourteen-volume encyclopaedia of Mamluk chancery practice. The work covers everything from geography and natural history to cryptography, heraldry, and administrative procedures. His chapter on cryptanalysis is one of the earliest systematic treatments of code-breaking.
bio: Egyptian scholar and administrator of the 14th-15th centuries known for his encyclopedic work on administrative science and geography. He served as a secretary and wrote extensively on chancellery practices and geographical knowledge.

school: Al-Khazini was a scientist of Byzantine Greek origin who worked in Merv under the Seljuqs. His 'Kitab Mizan al-Hikma' (Book of the Balance of Wisdom) is a landmark work on mechanics, hydrostatics, and the determination of specific gravities. He compiled remarkably accurate density tables for metals and gemstones and built precision balances that could detect differences of milligrams.
bio: Persian scientist of the 11th-12th centuries known for his work in physics, mathematics, and astronomy. He made significant contributions to the study of gravity, density, and mechanical instruments.

school: Abu al-Salt Umayya ibn Abd al-Aziz was an Andalusian polymath from Denia. He authored works on pharmacology, astronomy, geometry, and music theory. He spent years in Egypt and North Africa and his treatise on Egyptian materia medica and his astronomical tables contributed to the cross-pollination of Andalusian and Eastern Islamic sciences.
bio: Andalusian polymath of the 11th-12th centuries known for his work in astronomy, medicine, and mathematics. He made contributions to astronomical instruments and mathematical sciences in medieval Al-Andalus.

school: Ahmad ibn Yusuf ibn Ibrahim was an Arab mathematician whose treatise on ratios and proportions influenced medieval European mathematics through its Latin translation. He served the Tulunid rulers of Egypt and his work extended the Euclidean theory of proportionality. His commentary on Ptolemy's Centiloquium was widely read in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe.
bio: Arab mathematician of the 9th century known for his work on ratio and proportion theory. He contributed to geometry and the mathematical sciences during the Islamic Golden Age.

school: Habash al-Hasib al-Marwazi was a Central Asian astronomer and mathematician who worked at the Abbasid court. He compiled three astronomical handbooks (zij) and was one of the first to introduce the trigonometric functions of tangent and cotangent. His astronomical observations and computational methods influenced generations of Islamic astronomers.
bio: Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 9th century known for his astronomical observations and mathematical works. He made contributions to trigonometry and spherical geometry.

school: Ibn Yunus was an Egyptian astronomer who compiled the 'al-Zij al-Hakimi al-Kabir,' one of the most important astronomical handbooks of the medieval period. His remarkably precise observations of lunar and solar eclipses, conducted over many years at Cairo, were used by astronomers for centuries. He also developed new mathematical methods using prosthaphaeresis formulas.
bio: An Egyptian astronomer and mathematician of the 10th-11th centuries who made major contributions to trigonometry and spherical astronomy, and compiled important astronomical tables. He served under the Fatimid dynasty and developed significant trigonometric formulas.

school: Ibn al-Shatir was a Syrian astronomer and the chief muwaqqit (timekeeper) of the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus. He developed non-Ptolemaic planetary models using double epicycles that are mathematically identical to those later used by Copernicus. His work represents the culmination of a long tradition of Islamic critiques of Ptolemaic astronomy and may have influenced the Copernican revolution.
bio: A Syrian mathematician and astronomer of the 14th century who developed non-Ptolemaic astronomical models and made significant contributions to spherical trigonometry. His work influenced later astronomical thought.

school: Ibrahim ibn Sinan was an Arab mathematician and astronomer, the grandson of Thabit ibn Qurra. Despite dying at only 38, he made important contributions to the quadrature of the parabola, the theory of sundials, and the geometry of conic sections. He simplified his grandfather's proof of the area of a parabolic segment and developed elegant new methods.
bio: A 10th-century mathematician and astronomer from Baghdad, grandson of Thabit ibn Qurra, known for his work in geometry and astronomical calculations. He made contributions to the measurement of areas and volumes.

school: Kushyar ibn Labban Gilani was a Persian mathematician and astronomer whose astronomical tables and works on arithmetic influenced major figures including al-Biruni. His 'Principles of Hindu Reckoning' is an important source on the transmission of Indian mathematical methods into the Islamic world. He worked at the observatory in Rayy and contributed to the development of trigonometry.
bio: A Persian mathematician and astronomer of the 10th-11th centuries known for his works on arithmetic, geometry, and astronomy. He contributed to the development of mathematical and astronomical sciences in the Islamic world.

school: Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ma'ruf was an Ottoman polymath who built the great Istanbul Observatory in 1577, one of the largest astronomical observatories of its time. He developed mechanical clocks with minute hands, constructed astronomical instruments of unprecedented precision, and authored works on optics, mechanics, and steam power. The observatory was demolished in 1580 on the advice of the chief mufti.

Sharaf al-Zaman Tahir al-Marwazi was a Persian physician and natural historian who served at the court of the Seljuq sultan Malik-Shah. His 'Taba'i al-Hayawan' (Nature of Animals) contains important zoological observations and is a significant source on the natural history of Central Asia. He also left valuable geographic and ethnographic accounts of the peoples of Inner Asia.
bio: A Persian mathematician, astronomer, and geographer of the 11th-12th centuries known for his astronomical observations and geographical works. He made contributions to the science of geodesy and mapmaking.

school: Ahmad al-Farghani (Alfraganus) was a Central Asian astronomer who served at the Abbasid court of al-Ma'mun. His 'Jawami Ilm al-Nujum' (Elements of Astronomy) was one of the most widely read astronomical textbooks in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe. His calculation of the Earth's circumference influenced both Dante's cosmology in the Divine Comedy and Columbus's estimate of the distance to Asia.
bio: Persian astronomer of the 9th century known for his comprehensive astronomical treatise that became influential in both Islamic and European science. His work on celestial spheres was widely studied and translated.

school: Ibn al-Jazzar was a Tunisian physician from Kairouan whose 'Zad al-Musafir' (Provisions for the Traveller, known in Latin as Viaticum) became one of the most widely used medical textbooks in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe. He wrote on topics ranging from paediatrics to geriatrics and tropical diseases, and his practical approach to medicine influenced centuries of medical practice.
bio: A Tunisian physician of the 10th century known for his medical treatises and practical clinical observations. His works were widely used in medieval Islamic and European medicine.

school: Ali ibn Isa al-Kahhal was the foremost ophthalmologist of the medieval period. His 'Tadhkirat al-Kahhalin' (Memorandum for Oculists) described 130 eye diseases and their treatments with a precision that was not surpassed for centuries. Translated into Latin, the work became the standard ophthalmic textbook in European medical schools through the Renaissance.
bio: Arab ophthalmologist of the 10th century considered one of the greatest eye doctors of the Islamic Golden Age. He wrote influential treatises on eye diseases and surgical techniques.

books.title: Kitab al-Tasrif li-man Ajaza an al-Talif

school: Ali ibn al-Abbas al-Majusi (Haly Abbas) was a Persian physician whose 'Kamil al-Sina'a al-Tibbiyya' (Complete Book of Medical Art, known in Latin as Liber Regius) was the most comprehensive medical encyclopaedia before Avicenna's Canon. The work was celebrated for its clear organisation and practical clinical guidance and was widely used in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe.
bio: Persian physician of the 10th century known for his comprehensive medical encyclopedia that became influential in both Islamic and European medicine. His work synthesized Greek, Persian, and Indian medical knowledge.

school: Al-Samawal al-Maghribi was a mathematician of Jewish origin from Baghdad who later converted to Islam. His 'al-Bahir fi al-Jabr' (The Brilliant in Algebra), written when he was only 19, advanced the work of al-Karaji and introduced important methods for operating with negative numbers and polynomial arithmetic. He also worked on mathematical induction and combinatorics.
bio: Spanish-born Islamic mathematician of the 12th century known for his algebraic works and mathematical treatises. He made contributions to algebra and the theory of numbers.

school: Sinan ibn Thabit ibn Qurra was a Sabian physician and administrator who organised the hospital system of Baghdad under the Abbasid caliphs. He implemented the licensing of physicians, established a system of hospital inspections, and expanded medical care to prisons and remote areas. He also contributed to astronomy and mathematics, continuing the scientific tradition of his illustrious family.
bio: A 9th-10th century physician and mathematician, son of Thabit ibn Qurra, known for his medical treatises and astronomical work. He contributed to the advancement of medicine and mathematics in the Islamic world.
